translation: About what are they asking one another
commentary:
عَمَّ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ

About what are they asking one another? This opening question captures a scene of sarcastic mockery rather than genuine inquiry. The verb يَتَسَآءَلُونَ reveals people questioning each other, but their questions aren't sincere attempts to understand—they're condescending commentary designed to belittle what they've heard.

Understanding this distinction matters because questions serve two purposes: seeking knowledge or undermining someone's message. When someone asks "Seriously?" with a raised eyebrow, they're not looking for clarification—they're expressing disdain. This is what the Quraysh were doing when they heard about resurrection and accountability.

These people, whom the Quran calls المكذبين, didn't just reject the message—they actively campaigned against it. There's a profound difference between not believing something and calling someone a liar. If I present an argument and you walk away unconvinced, you haven't attacked me. But when you respond by attacking my character or trying to turn others against me, you've moved from neutrality to offense.

This distinction appears throughout Islamic history. The prophets faced not just disagreement but organized opposition designed to humiliate them and discredit their message. The Quraysh employed what we now call logical fallacies—rhetorical tricks that seem powerful but aren't based on sound reasoning. They would laugh dismissively, ask sarcastic questions about the details of Hell, or mock the very concept of resurrection. These tactics aimed to make the truth appear ridiculous without actually addressing its validity.

translation: About the great news
commentary:
عَنِ ٱلنَّبَإِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

About the Great News. Allah ﷻ doesn't leave us wondering what they're mocking—He immediately identifies it as النبأ العظيم, the momentous announcement.

The word نبأ differs significantly from خبر, the more common term for news. While khabar refers to any information—even trivial matters like "I'm lost, can you give me directions?"—naba' signifies consequential news that demands action. When Prophet Musa (AS) told his family, "Perhaps I will bring you some khabar," he was referring to simple information about directions. But when an army approaches or a natural disaster threatens, that's naba'—news that changes everything.

The word carries urgency. When a tsunami warning is issued and cities evacuate, that's naba'. You can't respond with casual interest; you must act. By calling resurrection naba', Allah ﷻ establishes that this isn't philosophical speculation to debate over tea—it's reality demanding preparation.

Adding عظيم intensifies the message further. Adheem means great, but it also implies something solid, unbreakable, firmly established. It's not subject to opinion or preference. Your feelings about a thunderstorm don't change whether it arrives. Similarly, your acceptance or rejection of resurrection doesn't alter its reality. The Day of Judgment stands as an immovable fact, regardless of human attitudes toward it.

This choice of adheem also emphasizes tangibility. Some imagine the afterlife as an ethereal, ghost-like existence—a mere state of mind. But the Quran presents Heaven and Hell as concrete realities, as real as the ground beneath our feet. The word adheem captures this solidity: resurrection isn't a metaphor or philosophical concept but an actual event in an actual place.

translation: That over which they are in disagreement
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ

The one about which they are in disagreement. This phrase adds a crucial dimension: their disagreement isn't new. The word مُخْتَلِفُونَ indicates ongoing, long-standing dispute. Different groups have held various views about the afterlife for generations—some believing in reincarnation, others in merging with nature, still others in complete annihilation at death.

But here's what makes this ayah striking: if they've always held different views without much fuss, why the sudden uproar? Why are they now asking these sarcastic questions about resurrection? Allah ﷻ points out the inconsistency in their behavior. They tolerated multiple conflicting beliefs for years, each group maintaining its own theory without attacking the others. But when Islam presented its view—a view that holds humans accountable for every action—they couldn't remain silent.

The answer lies in what Islam actually claims. The Quranic view of resurrection is the most demanding of all afterlife theories. It states that nothing we do goes unrecorded, that we'll face interrogation for our choices, that accountability is comprehensive and inescapable. For people who want to "just live their lives" without rules or consequences, this message is deeply threatening.

The disagreement itself reveals something important: Islam doesn't accommodate other views through vague pluralism. The Quran presents its message with authority, challenging every alternative understanding. It doesn't say, "You believe what you want, we'll believe what we want, and it's all equally valid." Instead, it demands evidence and engages directly with opposing views. This is verbal challenge, not physical confrontation, but it's challenge nonetheless.

## Ayahs 4-5

كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ
ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ

No! They will come to know. Then no! They will come to know. These two ayahs deliver Allah's response to the mockers with devastating brevity. He doesn't provide a point-by-point refutation of their arguments. Why? Because they didn't pose genuine arguments—they posed mockery. Mockery doesn't deserve rational response; it deserves to be put in its place.

The word كَلَّا functions as emphatic negation—an absolute "no" that refuses to engage with their nonsense. It's like responding to deliberate foolishness with "Absolutely not. We're not even going there." But kalla also carries forward momentum when connected to what follows: "Oh, they'll find out."

This isn't intellectual discourse anymore. It's a warning. Picture a student misbehaving in class, and the teacher says, "No, no, no—end of the day, we'll talk." That student's mind immediately races through every moment of misbehavior, knowing consequences await. That's the weight of سَيَعْلَمُونَ—"they will know."

But why repeat it? Why كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ followed by ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ? The Quran describes three types of warnings: the destruction of nations (which ended with the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ), the Day of Judgment, and Hellfire. The first warning came suddenly—people had no awareness before disaster struck. But the latter two involve knowing.

The first "they will know" refers to the Day of Judgment itself. When resurrection occurs, when people see their deeds recorded, when the trial begins—that's when initial realization hits. The criminal understands: "I'm guilty." But that's not the full realization.

The second "they will know"—preceded by ثُمَّ, indicating significant passage of time—refers to seeing Hell itself. Understanding guilt is one thing; seeing the prison that awaits is another level of comprehension entirely. That's when the full weight of their mockery and disbelief becomes devastatingly clear.

The repetition creates emphasis and also foreshadows the structure of what follows in the surah. Allah ﷻ will first describe signs pointing to resurrection, then describe Judgment Day, then describe Hell. Each level of knowing builds upon the previous one.

translation: No! They are going to know
Then, no! They are going to know
Have We not made the earth a resting place
commentary:
أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِهَـٰدًا

Have We not made the earth a resting place? Allah ﷻ changes approach entirely. Instead of continuing to address the mockers' attitude, He shifts to evidence from creation itself. But this isn't a dry scientific lecture—it's an invitation to reflection wrapped in beautiful comparison.

The word مِهَادًا refers to bedding, a cradle, something spread out for comfort. Parents make beds for their children; people prepare comfortable resting places. Allah ﷻ asks: Have I not made the entire earth into bedding? Your bed occupies a tiny space in your room. My bed accommodates all creatures—humans, animals, insects—across vast continents and ocean floors.

This establishes a pattern that continues through the following ayahs: Allah ﷻ compares human creation with divine creation. You make beds; I made the earth as a bed. Can you compare your handiwork with Mine? The question itself contains the answer. Human efforts, no matter how impressive, pale before divine creation.

The earth as mihad also implies stability and comfort. Despite spinning through space at tremendous speed, we don't feel it. Despite earthquakes and shifts in tectonic plates, the earth remains essentially stable enough for civilizations to flourish. This stability isn't random—it's deliberately designed for our benefit.

translation: And the mountains as stakes
commentary:
وَٱلْجِبَالَ أَوْتَادًا

And the mountains as stakes? The comparison continues. أَوْتَادًا refers to tent pegs, the stakes driven deep into ground to hold tents firm against wind. In ancient Arabian culture, tents were essential dwellings, and their stability depended entirely on properly placed pegs.

Mountains, when children draw them, look like tents, don't they? Allah ﷻ says: You make tents with pegs. When I make tents, they look like this—immovable, towering, permanent. When wind threatens your tents, they might collapse. When wind blows against My mountains, what happens? Nothing. They stand unmoved.

Modern geology has revealed that mountains have deep roots extending into the earth's crust, providing stability to continental plates. The Quran's description of mountains as "stakes" captures this function perfectly—they're not merely surface features but deeply anchored structures that serve essential purposes in the earth's stability.

The implicit question remains: Can you compare your construction with Mine? Your most impressive engineering projects crumble with time. Mountains stand for millennia. This comparison should humble anyone who witnessed it, reminding them of their limited power before the unlimited power of their Creator.

translation: And We created you in pairs
commentary:
وَخَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا

And We created you in pairs. The focus shifts from external creation to human existence itself. أَزْوَاجًا means pairs, but it also means groups or kinds. Both meanings apply here.

As pairs, humans require male and female for reproduction and survival. You didn't choose this arrangement; you were created into it. Aspects of your existence remain incomplete without partnership. Spiritual fulfillment, emotional wholeness, the continuation of humanity itself—all depend on this pairing. The Prophet ﷺ taught that marriage is half of one's religion, recognizing this fundamental incompleteness that only partnership addresses.

But azwaaj also means groups and societies. Human beings cannot survive alone. The person who claims "I don't need anyone" is either lying or hasn't faced serious hardship yet. We depend on each other for knowledge, for support, for companionship, for survival itself. People who isolate completely from society often develop unusual personalities—humans aren't designed for total solitude.

This ayah reminds the mockers: You didn't create yourself. You didn't design your own needs. You exist in a state of built-in incompleteness that requires others. Your very existence testifies to a Creator who designed you with specific needs and purposes.

translation: And made your sleep [a means for] rest
commentary:
وَجَعَلْنَا نَوْمَكُمْ سُبَاتًا

And We made your sleep as rest. The word سُبَاتًا comes from the root meaning to cut off or sever. Sleep cuts you off—from work, from problems, from consciousness itself. It's a temporary death from which you're resurrected each morning.

You love life. Given the choice, you'd probably want to live forever, experiencing everything, never stopping. But sleep forces you to stop. No matter how much you fight it, your body will surrender to sleep. Those hours you spend unconscious—think of all you could have done instead. You could have been working, earning money, spending money, traveling, enjoying yourself. But you can't, because sleep overtakes you without your permission.

This forced rest parallels death itself. Just as sleep comes whether you want it or not, death will come whether you're ready or not. The temporary cutting-off of sleep prefigures the final cutting-off of death. And just as you wake from sleep, resurrection promises awakening from death. Sleep becomes a daily reminder of human powerlessness and divine control over consciousness itself.

The comparison to Sabbath is apt—the Jewish day of rest also meant cutting off from work and worldly activities. That enforced rest, like sleep, reminds humans that they aren't meant to work endlessly. Built into creation itself is rhythm: activity and rest, work and recovery, life and death and life again.

translation: And made the night as clothing
commentary:
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ لِبَاسًا

And We made the night as clothing. This is among the most beautiful comparisons in these ayahs. Just as you cover your body with garments, Allah ﷻ covers the entire earth with night. Your clothing conceals and protects a small area—your physical form. His "clothing" conceals and protects the entire planet.

The word لِبَاسًا for clothing emphasizes the enveloping nature of night. When darkness falls, it wraps around everything. You can turn on lights inside your home, but those lights don't eliminate the overwhelming darkness outside. Night dominates despite human attempts to push it back with artificial illumination.

This garment of night serves essential purposes. It provides rest, coolness, privacy. Under cover of night, the earth recovers from the intensity of day. Nocturnal creatures emerge to play their roles in ecosystems. The rhythm of day and night maintains balance in ways we're only beginning to understand.

The comparison also highlights scale. You might feel proud of the nice clothes you wear. Allah ﷻ asks: Compare your garments to the garment I drape over the entire earth every day. Can your work match Mine?

translation: And made the day for livelihood
commentary:
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلنَّهَارَ مَعَاشًا

And We made the day for livelihood. The word مَعَاشًا refers to living, earning, sustaining life. Day becomes the time when the means of survival become available. Someone might claim they'll farm only at night—good luck with that. You need sunlight. Animals need sunlight. The plants need sunlight. All the mechanisms by which earth sustains life operate primarily during daylight hours.

Even in our modern world with its artificial lighting and 24-hour businesses, the day remains dominant for economic activity. Wall Street operates during the day. Banks open during the day. Most offices function during daylight hours. The "city that never sleeps" actually does sleep—visit after midnight and you'll find a fraction of the daytime activity.

Try to reverse it. Imagine if everyone slept during the day and worked only at night. It wouldn't work, because fundamental economic activities—agriculture, most manufacturing, most services—depend on daylight. Yes, some people work night shifts, but typically because better opportunities weren't available. The natural rhythm Allah ﷻ established makes day the time for ma'aash.

This pattern isn't random or cultural—it's built into creation itself. You cannot change it through preference or effort. The rhythm of day and night, work and rest, activity and recovery operates according to divine design, not human choice.

translation: And constructed above you seven strong [heavens]
commentary:
وَبَنَيْنَا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعًا شِدَادًا

And We constructed above you seven firm [heavens]. The comparison pattern continues: you have construction, you build buildings, you look admiringly at your ceilings and chandeliers. Allah ﷻ says: Look at My ceiling.

The word بَنَيْنَا means We built or constructed, using the same verb applicable to human building. But what was built? Seven heavens described as شِدَادًا—firm, strong, impenetrable, intense. Other ayahs ask: Do you see any cracks in that sky? Any openings? Any flaws? The construction is perfect and permanent.

Human buildings crack, decay, require maintenance, eventually crumble. The heavens above remain firm without any support we can see. No pillars hold them up, yet they don't fall. No maintenance crew repairs them, yet they remain intact. The construction is beyond human capability to even comprehend, let alone replicate.

The number seven appears throughout the Quran in reference to heavens, though the full nature of this remains beyond human perception. What matters here is the comparison: your most impressive architecture cannot approach the grandeur of what Allah ﷻ has constructed above you.

translation: And made [therein] a burning lamp
commentary:
وَجَعَلْنَا سِرَاجًا وَهَّاجًا

And We made a burning lamp. In that ceiling, Allah ﷻ placed a lamp—the sun. But this isn't an ordinary lamp. The word سِرَاجًا means a lamp or source of light, and وَهَّاجًا describes something that blazes intensely, repeatedly, continuously.

Wahhaaj implies that the burning doesn't diminish. The sun burns, and burns again, and burns again, maintaining consistent heat and light. Imagine if the sun's heat varied dramatically from day to day—what chaos would result on earth? Crops would fail, climates would destabilize, life would struggle to survive. But the sun maintains its output with remarkable consistency, day after day, year after year, millennium after millennium.

Compare your lamps—they burn out, require new bulbs, need electricity that comes from power plants that need fuel that eventually runs out. The sun requires no external fuel source we provide. It operates according to laws Allah ﷻ established, laws that maintain its burning without human intervention.

This brings the series of comparisons to a climax. From earth to mountains to sleep to night to day to the heavens to the sun—each comparison shows human creation and capability as weak and temporary compared to divine creation, which is powerful and permanent. The implicit question throughout has been: If you can't match My creation in these observable things, why do you think you can challenge Me regarding resurrection? If I can create all this, can I not recreate you after death? The argument answers itself through these powerful comparisons.

translation: And sent down, from the rain clouds, pouring water
commentary:
وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمُعْصِرَٰتِ مَآءً ثَجَّاجًا

Before examining this ayah, we must understand the remarkable pattern Allah ﷻ establishes throughout this passage. Everything mentioned comes in pairs – the earth with mountains, men with women, day with night, sky with sun. This consistent pairing carries profound meaning: when everything is created in pairs, why would this life not have its pair? Just as Allah ﷻ paired your body with your soul, He has paired this worldly life with the life to come.

Consider how Allah ﷻ describes the creative process itself. He mentions the earth (feminine in Arabic) paired with mountains (masculine) that hold it stable. Immediately after, He mentions men and women in pairs. At the beginning of the passage, we see the vast landscape with mountains as the prominent feature. At the end, we see the vast sky with the sun as its prominent feature – pair and pair, beginning and end.

When Allah ﷻ speaks of sleep in this passage, He describes it as something that "cuts you off" (قَطْع). Everything else appears in obvious pairs, but sleep's natural pair – waking up – remains unmentioned. Why? Because Allah ﷻ wants you to arrive at a deeper conclusion on your own. Sleep in the Quran is described as the closest experience to death. When we sleep and wake, we experience a miniature resurrection. This is why our morning supplication says, "Alhamdulillah alladhi ahyana ba'da ma amatana" – "All praise to the One who gave us life after He gave us death." We don't say "after He put us to sleep." The unstated pair to sleep is death, and the unstated pair to waking is resurrection. You should be intelligent enough to figure this out yourself.

Now we come to this ayah about rain. Allah ﷻ says He sends down from الْمُعْصِرَاتِ – those that squeeze. These are clouds pressing against each other, squeezing one another until their contents pour out. The word ثَجَّاجًا describes water that pours heavily and continuously, the torrential rain that results when clouds collide with thunderous sounds.

translation: That We may bring forth thereby grain and vegetation
commentary:
لِّنُخْرِجَ بِهِۦ حَبًّا وَنَبَاتًا

By means of this rain, Allah ﷻ brings forth حَبًّا – seeds that produce crops – and نَبَاتًا – all manner of vegetation. The earth transforms from barren to colorful, bursting with grass, weeds, trees and bushes of every kind.

translation: And gardens of entwined growth
commentary:
وَجَنَّـٰتٍ أَلْفَافًا

And gardens that are أَلْفَافًا – intertwined, folded into one another. The word لَفّ describes things so muddled together you cannot distinguish where one ends and another begins. Picture lush gardens where branches from different trees intertwine so completely that you cannot tell which branch belongs to which tree.

Notice something remarkable here: Allah ﷻ separates this description into distinct ayahs even though grammatically it forms one statement. The first ayah mentions حَبًّا وَنَبَاتًا – seeds and produce, which are necessities. Without crops, animals cannot eat, we cannot have vegetables or bread, life itself would stop. But then He adds in a separate ayah وَجَنَّـٰتٍ أَلْفَافًا – lush, beautiful gardens with sunlight beaming through leaves and branches. This is not necessity; this is added beauty.

When Allah ﷻ puts pairs together, two things emerge: purpose is fulfilled and beauty happens. Both are essential. These earthly gardens serve another purpose too – when you hear the word جَنَّات, what comes to mind? The gardens of Paradise. Allah ﷻ deliberately uses this word to remind you that your purpose is not merely to succeed in this life, but to reach far more beautiful gardens. These earthly gardens are just a preview.

Here's the profound point: this passage shows the sky and earth working together to produce new life. Water comes from the sky, falls on the earth, and together they generate vegetation. The sky and earth are paired, and they produce new life. Men and women are paired, and they produce new life. This entire worldly life, compared throughout the passage to a single day, is paired with another day – the Day of Judgment. When these two "days" come together, they will produce a new life: either Jannah or Jahannam.

Think about this from the perspective of the original audience – Arab travelers in the desert. A traveler sees the vast earth laid out before him like a bed. He sees mountains shaped like the pegs of his tent. As night falls, he pitches his tent and sleeps with his spouse – the night becomes like a garment (لِبَاس), just as the spouse is described as a garment elsewhere in the Quran. Sleep cuts him off from the world. Morning comes, he wraps up his tent and continues his journey under the endless desert sky with its blazing sun. By painting this scene of one night's journey, Allah ﷻ is actually describing all of human life. Your entire existence is like that one day of travel, and soon you will roll up your tent and depart. In fact, those mountains you thought were permanent fixtures will themselves be rolled up and sail away like mirages.

translation: Indeed, the Day of Judgement is an appointed time
commentary:
إِنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ كَانَ مِيقَـٰتًا

The Day of Separation (يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ) is a fixed appointment (مِيقَـٰتًا). The word فَصْل means to cut off, to distance, to separate – similar to the word قَطْع used earlier for sleep. But فَصْل also means to be decisive, final, with no going back.

Notice that Allah ﷻ calls this يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ rather than يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَامَةِ (the Day of Resurrection). This is strategic, because in the previous surah, Allah ﷻ asked the question, "What will give you any clue what Yawm al-Fasl is?" but didn't answer it. Now He answers that question, showing the continuity of the Quran's message.

Why call it the Day of Separation? Because on that day, you will be separated from everything: from your status in this world (whether you were wealthy, powerful or poor), from your wealth, from your friends, from your family, from all opportunities, from all the false hopes you lived with. Every time you turned a blind eye to sin and said "No big deal, Allah ﷻ will take care of it," you will be cut from all those delusions. 

Here's something profound: everything in this worldly life Allah ﷻ made in pairs. But the Day of Judgment is about no pairs – about being alone. That was the life of pairs; this is the life of separation.

translation: The Day the Horn is blown and you will come forth in multitudes
commentary:
يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَتَأْتُونَ أَفْوَاجًا

The day when "it" is blown into the horn (ٱلصُّورِ). Notice that "it" remains undefined – Allah ﷻ leaves the nature of this sound as a mystery, something almost beyond description. You will come أَفْوَاجًا – in massive groups, huge herds of people, coming forth submissively. The word أَفْوَاج suggests submission, like when the Quran says "The heavens and earth came to Allah ﷻ submissively."

A hadith in Bukhari provides striking detail: the Prophet ﷺ said the gap between the two blowings of the horn is forty. When the companions asked "Forty days?" he said "No." "Forty months?" "No." "Forty years?" He said "No" – just forty beyond description, eons upon eons that cannot even be defined. Then Allah ﷻ will send water from the sky, and people will emerge from the ground like crops sprouting. According to the hadith, nothing remains of the decayed human body except one tiny piece of the tailbone, perhaps even microscopic, and from that seed Allah ﷻ will resurrect everyone.

translation: And the heaven is opened and will become gateways
commentary:
وَفُتِحَتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَكَانَتْ أَبْوَٰبًا

The sky, which Allah ﷻ previously described as an impenetrable fort – seven solid heavens without a single crack – will be opened. فَكَانَتْ أَبْوَٰبًا – it will become nothing but doors, passageways, gates. Think of ancient city walls with massive gates through which armies would pass. The entire sky will transform into these floodgates, and through each one, armies of angels will pour down. The wall becomes all doors – no wall remains, just openings upon openings. The weakest part of any fortification is the door, and now the entire sky is just doors, completely broken open.

translation: And the mountains are removed and will be [but] a mirage
commentary:
وَسُيِّرَتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ فَكَانَتْ سَرَابًا

The mountains will be made to sail. Notice the passive form – we're not told who is moving them, creating a mystery. The word سَيْر means smooth, easy motion, like a leaf blowing gently down the street or someone strolling leisurely. These massive mountains, previously described as pegs driven into the earth, will move so smoothly they'll appear as سَرَابًا – a mirage.

Much of the Quran's description of cataclysmic Judgment Day events uses passive forms. "The earth will be stretched," not "Allah ﷻ will stretch the earth." "The mountains will be made to sail," not "Allah ﷻ will sail the mountains." This creates curiosity – people rising will ask "Why is this happening? Why is this happening?" Then they will know.

Elsewhere the Quran says: "You see the mountains thinking they are fixed, but they will sail like clouds." Someone who witnesses this moving will first deny it – "No, this cannot be happening" – and then suddenly the mountains themselves disappear, completely flattened. The mountains become so pulverized that no trace remains – not a single hill, not a single dip, completely flat.

For those of us who don't live in mountainous regions, we don't fully appreciate this. But when you stand on top of a mountain looking over the ocean, remembering that this impenetrable beast will be gone, completely leveled – it humbles you beyond words.

Why is the earth being flattened? Why is everything being leveled? First, it's making room for people, because every generation of humanity is being resurrected – billions upon billions. But also, room must be made for the scrolls, the records of deeds that will be laid out. These will be endless scrolls spread across the earth. The oceans will be dried up by making them boil over, all to create this space.

translation: Indeed, Hell has been lying in wait
commentary:
إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ كَانَتْ مِرْصَادًا

Jahannam (which is not originally an Arabic word but comes from Persian or Hebrew, meaning a valley of trash or a torture chamber) كَانَتْ مِرْصَادًا – has been waiting in ambush. The word مِرْصَاد comes from رَصَد, meaning to lie in wait to attack, to ambush. According to Imam ar-Razi, Hell has been waiting like a predator, asking "When will they arrive so I can finally feed?"

This connects to another scene in the Quran: when Allah ﷻ asks Jahannam "Are you full? Have you had enough?" it responds "هَلْ مِن مَّزِيدٍ – Is there any more?" Think about this: people are stuffed inside Hell, piled on top of each other with no room, crushing one another. When a new generation is thrown in, the people already there cry out "لَا مَرْحَبًا بِهِم" – not a welcome, but "We have no room here! What are you doing?" Hell itself is overstuffed, yet when Allah ﷻ asks if it has had enough, it demands more.

The word مِرْصَاد has another meaning: a road, a passageway. This creates two possible interpretations. The first is that Hell's guardians are waiting to ambush the disbelievers with punishment. The second interpretation is that everyone must pass over it – it is a road everyone must travel. The Quran says elsewhere, "There is not one of you except that you must go over it." We will all have to see it, to know what we've escaped. Some will keep falling in, while others will make it through to the gates of Heaven. This is why people walking toward Paradise keep saying "رَبَّنَا أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا – Our Lord, complete for us our light" – because it's completely dark and every step might be into empty space.

translation: For the transgressors, a place of return
commentary:
لِّلطَّـٰغِينَ مَـَٔابًا

For the الطَّاغِينَ (rebellious ones), it is a مَآب (place of return). The word طُغْيَان comes from طَغَى, used to describe water that goes out of reach, that floods beyond its container. Water has no respect – when a flood comes, it doesn't go around nice furniture, it takes your house without any consideration. It has no bounds until you put limits around it.

Allah ﷻ uses this word for people who rebel against Him. What does this mean? There are people who understand they should remain inside certain boundaries – don't do this haram, don't say these words, don't take that action, don't earn this kind of money. There are a few lines we're not supposed to cross. But they're like water that slips out and goes wherever it wants. These people who had free reign, who could do whatever they wanted, now get to go into Jahannam. Every time they try to get out, مَآب pulls them back in. This refers to them trying to scale out of Hell and being pushed back down, running out and being pushed back in. These were people who didn't want to stay within Allah's limits, and now they are eternally contained within the limits of Hell.

translation: In which they will remain for ages [unending]
commentary:
لَّـٰبِثِينَ فِيهَآ أَحْقَابًا

They will stay in it لَابِثِينَ – remaining for long periods – أَحْقَابًا – ages upon ages. The word لَبِث means to stay for an extended time. حُقْب (singular of أَحْقَاب) means an entire era, the time it takes for one generation to pass and another to come.

According to Ibn Abbas (RA), one huqb is approximately eighty years, but each year contains 365 days, and each of those days equals 1,000 years by our reckoning. Allah ﷻ doesn't say they'll stay for one huqb – He says ahqaban, huqb after huqb after huqb, on and on.

Why not just say they'll stay forever? Why break it up? Imagine telling a child he'll miss twenty minutes of recess, but only informing him of ten minutes at first. He watches the clock, counting down every second. The moment it hits ten minutes, you say "Actually, another ten minutes." The pain of that news, the hopelessness of knowing he must go back – those next ten minutes might as well be a thousand years. This is the psychological torture of Jahannam. After completing an entire huqb, a person might think they're getting an evaluation for early release, only to be thrown back down. مَآب – sent back again.

translation: They will not taste therein [any] coolness or drink
commentary:
لَّا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا بَرْدًا وَلَا شَرَابًا

They will not taste any بَرْدًا (coolness) in it. This means the intensity of the fire will never diminish. Normally with burn victims, the pain eventually lessens when nerve endings are destroyed. But the Quran says elsewhere, "We will replace them with new skin" – the pain never stops. They won't taste any شَرَابًا (drink) whatsoever.

translation: Except scalding water and [foul] purulence
commentary:
إِلَّا حَمِيمًا وَغَسَّاقًا

Except حَمِيمًا – scalding, boiling liquid – and غَسَّاقًا – puss, or melted copper. In Surah Ar-Rahman, Allah ﷻ completes this picture: there's fire burning, they cry for relief, for something to drink. They run to where there's liquid, but it's boiling puss and melted metal. "We can't take this anymore! We need to dry this off!" So they run back to the fire, then back to the drink, then back to the fire – يَطُوفُونَ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَ حَمِيمٍ آنٍ "making the rounds between it and scalding water."

translation: An appropriate recompense
commentary:
جَزَآءً وِفَاقًا

This is جَزَاءً – earned compensation, deserved payment. But Allah ﷻ adds وِفَاقًا, which means absolutely appropriate, perfectly fitting. The form of the word suggests this is the very definition of appropriate payment. If you ever think of what "appropriate compensation" means, this is it.

But the question arises: how could anyone deserve this? We only live on earth perhaps 80 or 90 years – how could any crime deserve huqb upon huqb of torture? Allah ﷻ answers this question.

translation: Indeed, they were not expecting an account
commentary:
إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَرْجُونَ حِسَابًا

These people لَا يَرْجُونَ حِسَابًا – they never expected, never hoped for, any kind of audit or accountability. This is the root problem before the crime itself.

These people lived with no sense of accountability whatsoever. Not just "I don't care about Hell or Judgment Day," but a complete absence of responsibility. When his mother said "Calm down, son," he said "I don't care." His brother was in the hospital – "I don't care." His mother begged him not to go to that party – "I don't care, Mom. Stop it. You don't own me."

No accountability for earning the right way, doing right by people, keeping promises, fulfilling responsibilities to family. No accountability for what they ate, what they did, who they were with. This is the contemporary YOLO lifestyle – "You Only Live Once" – the perfect translation of لَا يَرْجُونَ حِسَابًا. These were people who would explode if anyone tried to advise them. Anyone who softly counseled them was met with rage.

Their hopes and aspirations lay elsewhere. But that's not even the actual crime – that's just the foundation. The crime comes as a result.

translation: And denied Our verses with [emphatic] denial
commentary:
وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا كِذَّابًا

They declared Our ayat (signs and revelations) to be كِذَّابًا – complete lies. They didn't just deny them; they mocked them, ridiculed them, made a joke of them.

The word آيَات includes the revelations of Allah ﷻ, but it's broader. There are signs in your life that Allah ﷻ places to humble you. You're driving and there's an accident right in front of you. You were thinking of changing lanes seconds earlier but decided to wait two seconds, and those two seconds saved your life. Who put that thought in your head? That was a sign from Allah ﷻ. But you called that a lie too – you still didn't turn around and go home instead of going to that party.

كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا كِذَّابًا – they turned the signs into meaningless jokes, completely mocking them.

translation: But all things We have enumerated in writing
commentary:
وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَـٰهُ كِتَـٰبًا

And every single thing, We have completely recorded it, archived it, backed it up, into a كِتَابًا (record). 

When you study the Quran, remember there are two books: the Book of Revelation (the Quran) and the Book of Deeds. How you respond to that book determines what's recorded in these books. First you denied those revelations, and then that denial got recorded in these books.

The Quran says elsewhere, "We will write down what they say" (نَكْتُبُ مَا قَالُوا). Sometimes you say things – your father remembers, your mother remembers, your brother reminds you "I still remember that day you said that." But Allah ﷻ doesn't just remember that one day. Everything you say, He writes down. Every foul word, every lie, every word of oppression, every mean thing – everything is written.

Allah ﷻ records more than just words. He records how you looked, the way you stared at somebody, the expression on your face. He will ask you about the look on your face.

translation: So taste [the penalty], and never will We increase you except in torment
commentary:
فَذُوقُوا۟ فَلَن نَّزِيدَكُمْ إِلَّا عَذَابًا

Then taste this! We will not increase you in anything except عَذَابًا (punishment). You'll come back hoping for an increase in mercy – "No. We will give you nothing more except one thing: more punishment." Another huqb, another huqb, another huqb.

This horrifying description of Hell is then immediately followed by what we should be hopeful for.

translation: Indeed, for the righteous is attainment
commentary:
إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ مَفَازًا

No doubt about it – for the مُتَّقِينَ (those who protected themselves), there is مَفَازًا (ultimate success). The word مَفَاز captures both a time of success and a place of success. The time is the Day of Resurrection; the place is Jannah. Both meanings are crammed into one statement.

Previously, for the criminals, Allah ﷻ mentioned Judgment Day separately and Hell separately. For the believers, He combines them into one, describing how smooth the transition will be from Judgment Day into Jannah.

Allah ﷻ could have said "for those who believe," but instead He said مُتَّقِينَ – those who protected themselves. At the end of the day, the struggle to reach the Heaven that Allah ﷻ made for humanity is the struggle of deciding you're going to protect yourself.

Protect yourself from what? First, from the evil that lives inside you – the نَفْس, the greed, the lust. We are made of body and soul. Your body is an animal with appetites. It wants to eat, to fulfill its lusts. Your eyes want to look at attractive things. Your body calls for things, while your soul tells you "Stop. Protect yourself. You're not like an animal."

You can't just eat whatever you want, do whatever you want. You have to hold yourself back. If you keep giving into your body, your soul starts suffocating and you lose the ability to protect yourself. Like a muscle that weakens when you don't exercise it – if you stop exercising that ability to restrain yourself, it gets weaker and weaker until you're just an animal. In the Quran's language, your heart is dead. "إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ مَن كَانَ حَيًّا (He only warns those who are alive)" – if you're no longer alive, these words mean nothing.

translation: Gardens and grapevines
commentary:
حَدَآئِقَ وَأَعْنَـٰبًا

Large gardens (حَدَائِقَ) with varying colors, and grapes (أَعْنَابًا).

Is this the first time we're hearing about gardens in this surah? No – we already heard about gardens in this world. Allah ﷻ is taking us full circle. We expected Him to mention earthly gardens and then say "and the gardens of the Hereafter," but He reversed it, letting us know it's actually one for the other. He made this world beautiful because He wants you to go to Jannah. He described that horrible Hell in graphic detail because He doesn't want you to go there. It's better you know about it than experience it.

Why does the Quran describe Hell like this? The Bible doesn't. The Torah has no mention of Hell whatsoever. Allah ﷻ describes it in graphic terms so that at least now you know. Reality won't change just because you never read about it or heard about it.

That's why they can make cartoons mocking Hell, heavy metal music about "Highway to Hell," Halloween costumes with pitchforks and fake horns, treating it as a joke. For Muslims, Hell is never a joking matter. The Quran discussed it in a way that removes all humor from the subject. We can only take Hell seriously.

translation: And full-breasted [companions] of equal age
commentary:
وَكَوَاعِبَ أَتْرَابًا

And كَوَاعِبَ – perfectly matched spouses – أَتْرَابًا. The word أَتْرَاب means from the same soil as you, perfectly suited, exactly as you would want.

You could be attracted to someone, but when she opens her mouth, you want to jump off a cliff. Or everything might be wonderful, but there's no attraction. Or everything is good but there's this one thing you wish was different. There's no such thing as perfect in this life. Allah ﷻ says He will give you the exact kind of spouse perfectly, perfectly, perfectly suited for you.

This is mentioned because it represents man's greatest temptation. Allah ﷻ will take care of your wildest dreams.

translation: And a full cup
commentary:
وَكَأْسًا دِهَاقًا

And cups that are دِهَاقًا – splashing, overflowing.

What's the point of mentioning a splashing cup? Picture yourself in a lavish, elite gathering in a posh hall. Friends are having drinks, raising their cups in cheers, liquid splashing everywhere – and you don't care because nothing gets dirty in Jannah. Splashing drinks create an image of an incredible time, of pure enjoyment without consequences.

If you were to see this description – gorgeous gardens, beautiful companions, splashing drinks – and you were flipping channels, what would come next? أَسْتَغْفِرُ اللّٰه! But no – this is Heaven. It's going to be so much more beautiful than anything problematic in this world.

translation: No ill speech will they hear therein or any falsehood
commentary:
لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا كِذَّٰبًا

They will not hear any لَغْوًا (useless talk) in it, وَلَا كِذَّابًا (and no lies). This doesn't mean you only talk "strictly business." لَغْو means useless talk that doesn't serve any purpose, conversation just to pass time.

You know when families meet for a marriage proposal and they don't know how to start the conversation? "So, how's the weather in Chicago? I heard the airport is under construction." You didn't come here to talk about the airport. This is small talk to get to a point, and while it's happening, time slows down and you question your existence.

There's no such thing as boring conversations in Heaven. Everything you discuss will have meaning, will feel engaging and worthwhile. You won't have to hear those lies that were told about Islam, those hurtful things said about the Quran or about believers. All that كِذَّاب from before – none of that anymore.

This actually describes earthly gatherings where cups splash, where there's beauty and enjoyment, but where people talk trash about Islam. Sometimes you pass by those gatherings and you're the object of ridicule. Allah ﷻ says He will keep all the beautiful elements from those gatherings but remove everything that used to hurt you.

translation: [As] reward from your Lord, [a generous] gift [made due by] account
commentary:
جَزَآءً مِّن رَّبِّكَ عَطَآءً حِسَابًا

A جَزَاءً (recompense) from your رَبّ (Master). Notice He says رَبّ here, not just Allah ﷻ – you deserve this respect from your Rabb. The word used earlier for the criminals was just compensation, but this time it's different.

عَطَاءً means a grant, a gift. And حِسَابًا here means abundant, beyond calculation. Instead of Allah ﷻ checking "How many good deeds did you do? Okay, give him this many rewards," He will grant you out of pure generosity. He'll be generous in giving you bonuses. حِسَابًا basically means there's no accounting – just take, just enjoy, just enjoy.

translation: [From] the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the Most Merciful. They possess not from Him [authority for] speech
commentary:
رَّبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۖ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطَابًا

The Master of the heavens and the earth and whatever lies between them, الرَّحْمَـٰنِ – the Exceedingly Merciful.

We return to the skies and the earth, which should remind you of the entire passage about the sky and earth and their function, about how He made men and women, everything in pairs, just like He made that life and this life.

Why mention Ar-Rahman here specifically, not just Allah ﷻ? And He already mentioned رَبّ, so why add this attribute? Because that grant comes from Rahman. When He made these skies, He was Rahman. When He made the earth, He was Rahman. When He put everything between them, He was Rahman. When He places you in Heaven and gives you your cups and drinks, He is Rahman.

The same Rahman who created this world is the same Rahman treating you in Jannah. His entire goal in all of creation was to make you appreciate His mercy. His goal was never to put you in Hell – His goal was to be Rahman. That's why He takes you back to the skies and earth and whatever is between them, to this attribute of infinite mercy.

لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطَابًا – they will not possess from Him any speech. This connects to the final passage of the surah, which presents one of the grandest scenes of Judgment Day, bringing the entire surah together into a unified whole.

translation: The Day that the Spirit and the angels will stand in rows, they will not speak except for one whom the Most Merciful permits, and he will say what is correct
commentary:
يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلرُّوحُ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ صَفًّا ۖ لَّا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَقَالَ صَوَابًا

The image presented here is powerful: يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلرُّوحُ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ صَفًّا—the day when the angel and the angels stand in ranks. This is the posture of soldiers standing at attention before their commanding officer, legions of angels with their general—Jibreel (AS)—before them. These are the armies of heaven, assembled and disciplined.

Why are they standing there? Because when the Judge delivers His verdict, someone must escort the convicted to their sentence and welcome the innocent into Paradise. The angels who will serve the people of Jannah and those who will guard and punish the inmates of Jahannam—all of them now stand aligned in perfect order.

Then comes the restriction: لَّا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ—they do not speak. This includes even the recording angels who once sat on our shoulders documenting our every deed. Everything has been presented. The evidence is complete. No one speaks.

إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَقَالَ صَوَابًا—except the one Allah ﷻ has given permission to, and whoever that is will speak precisely and accurately. The word صَوَابًا comes from إِصَابَة, which means to hit a target exactly. There will be no verbose explanations, no building up to a point, no side commentary. In this world, we see lawyers in courtrooms going around and around a question, offering lengthy tangents to avoid the truth. On Judgment Day, there is none of that. When someone is given permission to speak, they will say exactly what needs to be said—nothing more, nothing less.

Who is granted this permission? One interpretation is that this refers to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who has been given the Maqaam Mahmood—the praised station—and will be granted the right to intercede. The hadith of intercession (Shafa'ah) describes this in detail: لَا يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ—no one can intercede except with His permission. And He ﷻ does grant His Prophet ﷺ that permission.

Another equally valid interpretation is that each angel, when called upon, will speak one at a time and deliver their testimony with precision before falling silent again. Allah ﷻ describes this elsewhere in the Quran: "Don't make arguments in front of Me." Anyone who speaks will give the right testimony—or even false testimony that reveals their true nature. Even liars will lie on that day, and their lies will serve as evidence against them. Some will claim, "By Allah, if You had guided me, I wouldn't be here!" And Allah ﷻ will call them out: "You're a liar."

This is the trial setting of Judgment Day.

translation: That is the True Day; so he who wills may take to his Lord a [way of] return
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ مَـَٔابًا

Allah ﷻ declares: ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلْحَقُّ—that day is the ultimate reality. The word الْحَقُّ carries profound meaning. It means truth, reality, and also right—as in the rights we owe one another. That day is when what is rightfully due to Allah ﷻ will be taken, and what you deserve will be given. It is the Day of Rights.

الْحَقُّ also means purpose. That day is the ultimate purpose for which we exist in this world. Everything we do amounts to something on that day. The true measure and reality of our actions will be revealed.

Consider: we all prayed together today. But will our prayers count the same before Allah ﷻ? Of course not. Some people who prayed here may have offered the prayer that earned them forgiveness and entry into Jannah. Others may have prayed while their minds wandered elsewhere, their intentions unfocused—perhaps they came only because someone made them. Their prayer, despite its outward form, may be worth nothing to Allah ﷻ.

The reality of what we do—the good and the bad—has an obvious external form and a hidden internal truth. The true value of everything will emerge on that day. Someone can be a scholar, an imam, a huffadh of the Quran, a giver of charity, a traveler in pursuit of knowledge—but all of these things can exist on the outside while something else entirely is happening on the inside. That day, the truth about who we really are will be exposed.

On the flip side, there are people who have little knowledge. They don't know Arabic, they can't recite Quran beautifully, they don't fit the outward image of piety we sometimes create—but they were sincere about Allah ﷻ. Whatever little they knew, they acted upon. They prayed with whatever words they had. And they may be worth more to Allah ﷻ on Judgment Day than someone who memorized the entire Quran but lacked sincerity.

Allah ﷻ sees their struggle. We don't see what He sees.

I once met a sister working as a flight attendant who recognized me. She came to me in tears and said, "I make istighfar every night. I've been looking for another job for three years. My parents are sick, my father needs medical attention, and this is the only job I could find to pay for his bills. I don't know what else to do. I cry every single night. When I'm done with work, I dress the way I'm supposed to—I put on my hijab. But they told me they'll fire me if I wear it at work." And she said, "I know I'm a sinner."

There are people living in comfort who walk into haram and celebrate it. This woman is cornered, trying to care for her parents, and she's violating some rules of the religion—but she's making istighfar and crying before Allah ﷻ every night. How does Allah ﷻ value people like her? We cannot judge. Only He knows the full reality.

Then Allah ﷻ says: فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ مَـَٔابًا—whoever wants may take a road back toward their Master. The word مَـَٔابًا means to return. This implies that we were originally in Allah's company—He introduced Himself to our souls before we came to this world—and now He's saying: "Come back to Me." Allah ﷻ is not a new discovery. You already knew Him. You've simply forgotten. Come back.

This is the meaning of tawbah. It doesn't just mean repentance—it literally means to return to Allah ﷻ.

This is the second time the word مَـَٔابًا appears in this surah. The first time was in the description of Jahannam, where people are thrown back into the fire over and over again. Allah ﷻ is saying: that's not the ma'ab I want for you. I want you to run back to Me, to make your Rabb your place of return.

translation: Indeed, We have warned you of a near punishment on the Day when a man will observe what his hands have put forth and the disbeliever will say, "Oh, I wish that I were dust
commentary:
إِنَّآ أَنذَرْنَـٰكُمْ عَذَابًا قَرِيبًا يَوْمَ يَنظُرُ ٱلْمَرْءُ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ وَيَقُولُ ٱلْكَافِرُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ تُرَٰبًۢا

Allah ﷻ declares: إِنَّآ أَنذَرْنَـٰكُمْ—We have thoroughly warned you. This is an emphatic statement: no one has warned you like We have warned you of a punishment that is very near.

What is that punishment? The Day of Judgment. The day when every single person—مَرْء includes men and women—will take a good, hard look at what their two hands sent forward. مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ—what did your hands put forward?

This is actually farming terminology. A farmer takes a seed, places it in the ground with his hands, and doesn't see the result for months or even a year. He has sent it ahead, investing in the future. Every deed we perform is like planting a seed—an investment that will bloom on Judgment Day. Every single one.

Take stock of your life. How many years do you have left? What have you done for Allah ﷻ? How many seeds have you planted? This is a serious question. We audit our careers, our finances, our goals—but have we done an audit for Allah ﷻ? What have we actually accomplished for Him? We're heading to that meeting whether we like it or not. The appointment is set. Are we ready?

Then Allah ﷻ describes the reaction of the disbeliever: وَيَقُولُ ٱلْكَافِرُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ تُرَٰبًۢا—the ingrate will say, "Oh, if only I was just dirt!" In other words, if only I had never been resurrected. If only my wish that there was no afterlife had been true, then I could have stayed as dust. Why did there have to be a resurrection? Can't I just stay in the grave? Can You just kill me again, please?

But Allah ﷻ responds elsewhere in the Quran: Don't beg for one death—beg for death over and over again. And We'll tell them, "Why are you begging for death? You won't get to live, and you won't get to die. That's your reality now."

Here, the disbeliever wishes he could remain forgotten, decayed, turned to dust. That would be so much better than what he now faces.

---

**The Architecture of Surah An-Naba**

As we conclude this surah, it's worth reflecting on its beautiful structure. In my estimation, Surah An-Naba can be broken into five distinct parts:

1. **The Opening Question**: People sarcastically questioning Judgment Day—"What are they asking about?"

2. **The Evidence of Creation**: Allah ﷻ points to life created in pairs, the mountains, the earth, sleep as rest, the night as a covering, the rain, and the gardens—all signs pointing to the reality of resurrection.

3. **The Day of Separation**: Allah ﷻ describes Judgment Day itself—the mountains sailing, the sky torn apart, the appointed time that has been set.

4. **The Two Destinations**: Heaven and Hell are described in vivid detail—there are only these two outcomes.

5. **Humility on Judgment Day**: Angels standing in silence, no one speaking except by permission, and the disbeliever wishing he was dust.

Notice the symmetry: the surah begins with people being obnoxious about Judgment Day, mocking it with sarcasm. By the end, everyone is humbled. No one speaks out of turn. Even the disbeliever says the right thing in acknowledging his regret. لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطَابًا...وَقَالَ صَوَابًا. The tables have completely turned. Allah ﷻ said at the beginning, "They will know"—and by the end, they do know.

The second passage described this life in pairs. By contrast, the fourth passage shows that in the next life, those pairs are separated—you're either in Jannah or Jahannam. Right in the middle of these is the argument for Judgment Day itself. Everything hangs in the balance of that day—it evaluates the previous life and determines the reward of the next.

So we have perfect symmetry: this life, Judgment Day, next life—all centered on the Day of Accountability. The surah began with questioning Judgment Day, ended with the humility of Judgment Day, and placed the description of Judgment Day at its very center.

This is the literary perfection of the Quran, designed to help us appreciate the beauty and power of Allah's Word.
